title: Naked Single
description: When a cell has only ONE candidate left, that must be the answer!
concept: After eliminating all numbers that appear in the same row, column, and 3x3 box, if a cell has only ONE candidate remaining, that is the only possibility.

steps.text: Welcome to your first Sudoku lesson! 🎉 Look at the empty cells — each one shows tiny 'pencil marks' (candidates). These are all the numbers that COULD go in that cell.
A 'Naked Single' happens when all numbers except ONE have been eliminated from a cell. You can see it in the pencil marks — the cell has only one tiny number left!
Let's look at row 3 (highlighted in blue). It already has 3, 4, 6 filled in. The empty cells need: 1, 2, 5, 7, 8, 9.
Look at this cell's pencil marks. Check what numbers are already in its row, column, AND 3x3 box...
After checking row + column + box, most numbers are eliminated. If only ONE candidate remains, that's a Naked Single! 🎯
A Naked Single is the simplest technique — when only one number can possibly fit. As you solve more cells, more naked singles appear! ✅

title: Hidden Single
description: When a number can only go in ONE place within a row, column, or box.
concept: Even if a cell has multiple candidates, a specific number might be 'hidden' — it can only go in ONE cell within a row, column, or box.

steps.text: Great job on your White Belt! 🌟 Now let's learn 'Hidden Single'. A number can only go in ONE cell in a row/column/box, even though that cell has other candidates.
Look at row 1 (top row). It's completely empty! All 9 numbers need to be placed. Let's focus on where specific numbers can go.
Check the first 3 cells — they're in box 1 (top-left). Now check column by column: which columns already have a 1 placed somewhere?
Column 3 has a 1 (row 4). Column 5 has a 1. So in row 1, the number 1 can't go in columns 3 or 5. Keep eliminating!
After checking ALL columns and the box constraints, there's only ONE cell in row 1 where 1 can go. The number is 'hidden' among many candidates! 🎯
Hidden Singles are everywhere once you learn to spot them! Scan each row, column, and box asking: 'Where can number X go?' If only ONE place — that's your answer! ✅

title: Naked Pair
description: When two cells in the same group have the same two candidates, eliminate those numbers from other cells.
concept: If two cells in a row, column, or box have exactly the same two candidates, those two numbers MUST occupy those two cells. Remove them from all other cells in the group!

steps.text: Orange Belt time! 🧡 The Naked Pair is one of the most useful techniques. It lets you eliminate candidates even when you can't place a number yet.
Look at row 1 — these two highlighted cells. Check their pencil marks. They both have the same two candidates!
Since both cells can ONLY be 1 or 6, one must be 1 and the other must be 6. We don't know which is which — but we know those numbers are LOCKED into these two cells.
Since those numbers must go in the yellow cells, we can ELIMINATE 1 and 6 from all other cells in the same row! This narrows down their candidates.
These two cells are ALSO in the same 3x3 box! So we can eliminate from other cells in the box too. One elimination leads to another! 🎯
🎉 Naked Pairs are powerful! Find two cells with the same 2 candidates, then eliminate those numbers from peers. This often creates new Naked Singles nearby!

title: Hidden Pair
description: When two numbers can only go in the same two cells, those cells form a hidden pair.
concept: If two numbers can only appear in the same two cells within a group, then those cells can only contain those two numbers. Remove all OTHER candidates from those cells!

steps.text: Staying at Orange Belt! 🧡 Hidden Pairs are the opposite of Naked Pairs. Instead of finding cells with matching candidates, we find numbers that share the same two cells.
Imagine checking: 'Where can the number 6 go in this box?' And: 'Where can the number 7 go?' If BOTH numbers can ONLY go in the same two cells — that's a Hidden Pair!
Look at row 4. It has many candidates. Let's check where numbers 6 and 7 can go in this row.
Check the pencil marks carefully. Numbers 6 AND 7 can only appear in these two highlighted cells in this row. No other cell in the row can hold 6 or 7!
Since 6 and 7 must occupy these two cells, we can remove ALL other candidates from them. The hidden pair {6,7} is revealed!
🎉 Hidden Pairs clear away extra candidates, often revealing naked singles! The trick: scan for two numbers that share only two possible cells in any group.

title: Pointing Pair / Triple
description: When candidates in a box are aligned on one row or column, eliminate them from the rest of that row/column.
concept: If all instances of a number within a box fall on the same row (or column), that number MUST be in that row within the box. So it can't appear anywhere else on that row outside the box!

steps.text: Green Belt! 🟢 Pointing Pairs connect boxes to rows/columns. When all candidates of a number in a box line up on one row, they 'point' at candidates to eliminate outside the box.
Look at row 3. We're going to check where a specific number can go in this row and its intersecting boxes.
In box 3 (top-right), the number 3 only appears in these two cells — both on the same row! The pair 'points' along the row.
Since 3 MUST be in one of the yellow cells (it's the only place in box 3), 3 can be eliminated from these other cells in the same row!
The same logic works for columns: if all candidates of a number in a box line up on one column, eliminate that number from the rest of the column outside the box.
🎉 Pointing Pairs bridge boxes and rows/columns! Find a number constrained to one line in a box, then eliminate it from the rest of that line. Very powerful!

title: Box/Line Reduction
description: When candidates in a row or column are all in the same box, eliminate them from the rest of the box.
concept: The reverse of Pointing Pairs! If all candidates of a number in a row (or column) fall within the same box, that number can't appear anywhere else in that box.

steps.text: Still Green Belt! 🟢 Box/Line Reduction is the flip side of Pointing Pairs. Instead of box pointing at a row, the row points at a box.
Look at row 1. Check where the number 2 can go in this row. Look at the pencil marks...
All the candidates for 2 in row 1 are in these two cells — both in box 2 (top-center)!
Since 2 must go somewhere in row 1, and it can only go in box 2, then 2 in box 2 is already accounted for by this row. No other cell in box 2 can be 2!
We can eliminate 2 from other cells in box 2 (highlighted in red). The row 'locks' 2 into just these positions in the box.
🎉 Box/Line Reduction clears candidates from a box when a row or column constrains them. Combined with Pointing Pairs, these are your intersection tools!

title: Naked Triple
description: When three cells in a group share exactly three candidates total, eliminate those numbers from other cells.
concept: Like Naked Pairs but with three cells and three candidates. The three cells don't each need all three numbers — they just need to contain ONLY from the same set of three numbers in total.

steps.text: Blue Belt! 🔵 Naked Triples extend the Pair concept to three cells. Three cells that share exactly three candidate numbers total — those numbers are locked in!
Look at these three cells in the center row. Check their pencil marks carefully.
A Naked Triple can be: {5,8,9} {5,8} {5,9} — each cell has different candidates but together they only use three numbers total.
These three cells collectively contain only {5, 8, 9}. So 5, 8, and 9 are LOCKED into these three cells in this row.
We can eliminate 5, 8, and 9 from all other cells in the same row! This often triggers a chain reaction of simpler deductions.
🎉 Naked Triples follow the same logic as Pairs — just with three numbers across three cells. The key: in total, only three unique candidates across all three cells.

title: Hidden Triple
description: When three numbers can only go in three cells, remove other candidates from those cells.
concept: The hidden version of Naked Triples. If three specific numbers can only appear in three cells within a group, remove all OTHER candidates from those cells. The triple was 'hidden' by extra candidates.

steps.text: Staying Blue! 🔵 Hidden Triples are trickier to spot. Instead of three cells with three matching candidates, we find three numbers that can only go in three cells.
Look at row 1 — it has many candidates. We need to find three numbers that share exactly three cells.
Check the pencil marks for these three cells. Look for three specific numbers that can ONLY appear in these cells in row 1.
The numbers {2, 5, 6} can only go in these three cells — nowhere else in row 1! Even though each cell has other candidates too, those extra candidates are hiding the triple.
Since {2, 5, 6} must go in these three cells, we can remove ALL other candidates from them! The hidden triple is revealed.
🎉 Hidden Triples clear away disguise candidates, often leaving naked pairs or singles behind! Harder to spot but very rewarding.
